aliases: Root beer and vanilla ice cream float

english: Root beer and vanilla ice cream float

food_subgroup: Soft drinks and ice cream beverages

ontology.foodex2_description: Soft drinks and similar beverages — ice cream-based beverage
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for root beer float specifically; A0CKP (soft drinks category) used as closest defensible anchor. Root beer float is a composite beverage of carbonated root beer and vanilla ice cream.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for root beer float as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard root beer float is typically served in a tall glass of approximately 400 ml, consisting of carbonated root beer poured over one to two scoops of vanilla ice cream.

nutrient_source.title: Root Beer Float — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Root Beer Float (cr_id D06020029). Values represent the blended beverage as consumed. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for vanilla ice cream (NDB 19095) proportionally adjusted to match the HPB total fat figure, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; ice cream float / sweetened carbonated beverage with ice cream analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for root beer float in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 68 (medium) based on the weighted composite of: (1) carbonated sugar-sweetened beverage GI ~65 (University of Sydney GI Database, glucose reference) and (2) vanilla ice cream GI ~62 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021). The beverage is approximately 80% root beer and 20% ice cream by weight in a standard serving. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as: (68 × 18.82 × 4.0) / 100 = 51.2 g. This is a high glycemic load per serving despite a medium GI, driven by the very large carbohydrate mass per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (18.52 g/100 g) driving insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain; negligible potassium and dietary fibre
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid root beer floats entirely, as the 18.5 g/100 g sugar load — delivering approximately 74 g sugar per standard 400 ml serving — promotes insulin-driven sodium retention, weight gain, and endothelial dysfunction, all of which independently elevate blood pressure; unsweetened sparkling water with fresh fruit is a suitable alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high sugar content (18.52 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre, producing a rapid and severe postprandial blood glucose spike; estimated GI 68 with glycemic load of 51.2 g per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Root beer float is strongly contraindicated for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its 18.5 g/100 g sugar content, zero dietary fibre, and a glycemic load of approximately 51 g per standard serving, which causes a rapid and severe postprandial blood glucose spike; if occasionally desired, substituting diet root beer and a small portion of low-sugar frozen yogurt with careful blood glucose monitoring is the only partially mitigating strategy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar intake (18.52 g/100 g) linked to hypertriglyceridaemia, hepatic de novo lipogenesis, and increased cardiovascular risk; saturated fat from ice cream component contributes to LDL elevation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid root beer floats, as the high free sugar content (18.5 g/100 g, approximately 74 g per standard serving) promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and hepatic de novo lipogenesis, while the saturated fat from the ice cream component contributes to LDL cholesterol elevation, collectively increasing atherosclerotic cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar (18.52 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; saturated fat from ice cream further amplifies RA-relevant inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid root beer floats, as the 18.5 g/100 g refined sugar content promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production and the saturated fat from the ice cream component amplifies RA-relevant inflammatory pathways; anti-inflammatory beverages such as green tea or ginger tea are strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A root beer float is a classic American dessert beverage consisting of carbonated root beer poured over one or more scoops of vanilla ice cream, served in a tall glass. The drink originated in the United States in the late 19th century and remains a popular nostalgic treat. As the root beer contacts the ice cream, it creates a frothy foam on the surface and a creamy, effervescent mixture below. The flavour profile combines the sweet, herbal, and slightly medicinal notes of root beer — typically flavoured with sassafras, vanilla, and wintergreen — with the rich, creamy sweetness of vanilla ice cream. The texture is simultaneously fizzy and creamy, with the ice cream slowly melting into the beverage as it is consumed. Per 100 g, the root beer float provides 76 kcal, 0.29 g protein, 0.44 g fat, and 18.82 g carbohydrate of which 18.52 g is sugar, with negligible dietary fibre (0.0 g) and low sodium (6 mg). The very high sugar content and near-zero fibre make this beverage clinically significant: it is strongly contraindicated for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. A standard 400 ml serving delivers approximately 74 g of free sugar and a glycemic load of approximately 51 g, representing a severe metabolic burden. The food is vegetarian but not vegan due to the dairy ice cream component, and contains milk allergens. It is classified as ultra-processed.
common_user_queries: What is a root beer float?
How many calories in a root beer float?
Is root beer float suitable for diabetics?
Can I drink root beer float if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D06020029, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy vanilla ice cream entry proportionally adjusted to match HPB total fat. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous foods (sugar-sweetened carbonated beverage and vanilla ice cream) with documented weighting rationale. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as root beer float contains no dietary fibre by composition (carbonated sugar water and ice cream provide no fibre). 2. calcium_mg and iron_mg are confirmed zero per HPB source (D06020029). 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this composite beverage is not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for root beer float (D06020029) as of 2026-04-16, and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy does not provide a composite root beer float entry with full micronutrient breakdown. These fields are therefore set to -1 to indicate data not available from any approved source consulted. 4. GI value of 68 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sugar-sweetened carbonated beverages (~65) and vanilla ice cream (~62), weighted approximately 80:20 by beverage composition; no direct GI measurement for root beer float was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 5. Glycemic load per 400 g standard serving = (68 × 18.82 × 4.0) / 100 = 51.2 g — this represents a very high glycemic load per serving. 6. Saturated fat (0.27 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy vanilla ice cream (NDB 19095) proportionally adjusted to match the HPB total fat of 0.44 g/100 g; this is an adapted value and not directly reported by HPB. 7. The input record contained inaccurate dietary_tags including 'wholegrain', 'low_sodium', and 'low_calorie' — these have been removed as they are factually incorrect for root beer float. 8. The input occasion_tags included 'breakfast' — this has been reclassified to 'dessert', 'snack', and 'treat' as root beer float is not a breakfast food. 9. Halal status is marked false as commercial root beer may contain trace alcohol from natural flavouring fermentation; consumers should verify halal certification on specific products.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Documented all -1 fields in 'notes' with reason for data unavailability and source consulted, resolving g3_numeric_hygiene failure from iteration 1.

Iteration 3: Expanded g3 documentation in 'notes' to explicitly name the approved sources consulted (HPB Foodo D06020029 and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy) that confirmed unavailability of detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this composite beverage, ensuring full compliance with the negative sentinel values rule.
